title: Evaluating Translation Quality
concept: BLEU is the standard precision metric, but modern systems also use COMET to compare neural embeddings of translation outputs against human references.

exercises.prompt: How do computer scientists automatically test if an AI translation system is accurate?

options.text: By comparing the computer's translation against a human reference translation
By counting how many seconds the translation takes
By checking if the translation has any spelling errors

exercises.explain: BLEU is a metric that scores translations by checking how many words match a human-written reference translation.

cards.title: Who Grades the Translator?
cards.body: How do we know if a machine translator (like Google Translate) is actually doing a good job? We can't hire humans to grade millions of sentences every day. Instead, we use smart math and AI helper models to grade them automatically!

cards.title: BLEU vs. COMET
cards.body:
To evaluate how good an AI's translation is, we use metrics. 

**BLEU** is the traditional standard. It calculates *precision* by counting how many words in the AI's translation match the human reference translation exactly. 

**COMET** is a modern metric that uses neural networks to compare the *meaning* (embeddings) of the AI's translation against the human reference, even if the exact words are different.

cards.title: The Recipe Test
cards.body:
Imagine grading a translation like comparing two recipes. 

BLEU is like a robot checking if the exact ingredients (words) match. COMET is like a professional food critic who tastes both dishes. Even if the critic's dish uses slightly different ingredients (synonyms), they can tell if it tastes just as delicious (means the same thing).

cards.title: Spotting the Difference
cards.body:
Let's look at a translation of the French phrase: *"Il fait beau."*
*   **Human Reference:** "The weather is beautiful."
*   **AI Translation A:** "The weather is lovely."
*   **AI Translation B:** "The is weather beautiful."

**BLEU** would penalize Translation A because "lovely" doesn't match "beautiful" exactly, even though it's a great translation! 

**COMET** would recognize that "lovely" and "beautiful" have very similar neural embeddings, giving Translation A the high score it deserves.

cards.title: The Perfect Match Myth
cards.body:
**Myth:** If a translation gets a low BLEU score, it must be a terrible translation.
**Fact:** Not necessarily! Because BLEU looks for exact word matches, a translation that uses different but correct synonyms (like "couch" instead of "sofa") will get a low BLEU score, even if it is perfect. That's why we need COMET!

cards.title: Side-by-Side Evaluation
cards.body: See how the two metrics evaluate the same translation differently when synonyms are used.

right.text: Looks at neural embeddings. Recognizes that 'huge' and 'large' mean the same thing.

left.text: Looks for exact word overlap. Misses points for synonyms like 'huge' instead of 'large'.

exercises.prompt: How does the classic BLEU metric calculate the quality of a translation?

options.text: It counts the exact word matches between the translation and the reference.
It translates the text into multiple languages at the same time.
It interviews human judges to get their personal opinions.
It generates new sentences from scratch using neural networks.

exercises.explain: BLEU is a precision-based metric that focuses on counting exact matching words or phrases between the candidate translation and the reference translation.

exercises.prompt: True or False: COMET can give a high score to a translation that uses synonyms instead of the exact words in the human reference.
exercises.explain: This is true! Because COMET compares neural embeddings (meanings) rather than exact words, it can recognize that two sentences mean the same thing even if they use different words.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of how COMET works.
exercises.sentence: Unlike BLEU, COMET uses neural [blank] to compare the meaning of a translation to the reference.
exercises.explain: COMET uses neural embeddings (mathematical representations of meaning) to compare sentences, allowing it to understand synonyms and sentence structure.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following statements about translation metrics is FALSE?

options.text: BLEU is better at understanding synonyms than COMET.

exercises.explain: The third option is incorrect because BLEU is the one that looks for exact words, whereas COMET looks at the underlying meaning (embeddings).

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a new translation app. Why would you choose to use COMET alongside the classic BLEU metric to test your system?

options.text: BLEU is always better because it understands human emotion and sarcasm.
COMET only counts exact word matches, making it much faster than BLEU.
BLEU checks for exact word matches, while COMET evaluates the similarity of meaning using neural embeddings.
Neither metric can be used to compare machine translations against human references.

exercises.explain: BLEU looks for exact word matches (precision) which can miss good translations using synonyms. Modern systems use COMET because it compares the deeper meaning (neural embeddings) of the translations against the reference.
